pages.right: 0. 什么都不是，只是个女人。

pages.left:
1. Yiping’s Death

By the time I began writing these stories, Yiping was already dead.
pages.right:
1. 怡萍之死

当我写下这些故事的时候，怡萍已经死了。

pages.left:
2. Question Marks

This is an age full of question marks.

Wang Yiping was sitting at the entrance of a public restroom, smoking. A man who had just finished peeing came out, fastening his belt as he walked, and stepped on the hem of her skirt. Yiping held the cigarette between her lips and calmly brushed the dust off her skirt with her hand. Before she could clean it completely, ash from the burned-out cigarette dropped down and dirtied it again.

At that moment a little boy came over. His eyes were wide. He stood in front of her and said,

“Auntie, girls aren’t allowed to sit with their legs spread like that. My mom told my sister that.”

Yiping slowly exhaled a puff of smoke and replied,

“Did your mom ever tell you that when she gave birth to you, her legs were spread just like this?”

The boy looked half confused, half understanding. His eyes were full of question marks.

Yiping crushed the cigarette out beside her skirt and said to him,

“You should respect every woman who dares to spread her legs. For them, it’s only a passage.”

Across from the restroom, the construction site began roaring again. The crane lifted and lowered its load. A gust of wind came and flipped Yiping’s skirt up, revealing pale pink underwear at the top of her thighs, caught with a few stray hairs.

The boy stared blankly at the scene before him.

Yiping ground the cigarette butt hard beneath her foot and thought:

Damn it. Every single one of them ought to be hanged by a crane.
pages.right:
2. 问号

这个是充满问号的时代 王怡萍坐在公共厕所门口抽烟的时候有个尿完尿的男人走出来边系裤腰带边踩了她的裙子一脚 王怡萍叼着烟 很平静地伸手拍了拍裙摆的灰 还没擦干净 烟头烧完的灰掉下来 又弄脏了 这时候走过来个小男孩睁着大眼睛 站在她面前对她说 阿姨 女生穿裙子腿不可以分得这么开 我妈妈就是这么对姐姐说的 王怡萍依旧很平静地吐了口烟 对小男孩说 那你妈妈有没有告诉过你 生你的时候腿就张得这么开 小男孩一脸似懂非懂 眼里有问号 王怡萍把烟直接在裙子旁边捻灭 对小男孩说 你得尊重每一个敢把腿张开的女人 因为对她们来说 那只是个通道 公厕对面的工地又开始轰隆隆地施工 起重机抬起来又放下 一阵风刮来把王怡萍的裙子吹得翻飞 露出下面腿根边缘夹着几根毛的肉粉色内裤 小男孩痴痴呆呆地看着眼前的风景 王怡萍又把烟头狠狠踩了一脚 心想 他妈的 一个两个的 都该被起重机吊死.

pages.left:
3. The Green Poplar

When Wang Yiping was six years old, a female teacher once told her:

“Yiping, you’re a girl. You can’t wipe your snot on your skirt.”

Yiping pointed at a boy next to her who was stuffing the finger he had just used to wipe his nose into his mouth.

“Why can he?”

The teacher glanced over and said,

“He’s a boy. Boys can be a little dirty.”

Yiping said,

“Then I want to be a boy too.”

The teacher burst out laughing and patted Yiping’s little braid.

“That’s impossible. When you grow up you’ll be a mother.”

Yiping’s eyes widened in horror.

“I don’t want to be a mother! Mothers have to clean all the time. I hate cleaning. And mothers are boring—they don’t know anything. Every time I ask my mom a question she tells me to ask my dad.”

The teacher had nothing to say. She could only pat Yiping’s head and urge her,

“It’s nap time. Go lie down.”

Yiping was pressed along by the shoulders, following behind a crooked line of stumbling children into a large room where rows of tiny beds were arranged neatly. The room smelled sour—sweat, milk, someone’s stinky socks, and a bed sheet someone had wet.

It smelled awful.

But Yiping was used to it.

She found her little bed and lay down numbly, though she wasn’t actually sleepy. Listening to a child with sinus problems sniffling endlessly, she stared blankly out the large glass window at the enormous green poplar tree outside.

She thought:

The sunlight and leaves out there look so fresh. Why do they have nothing to do with me?
pages.right:
3. 绿色杨树

王怡萍六岁那年 有个女老师对她说 怡萍 你是个女孩子 不能把鼻涕抹在裙子上 王怡萍指了指旁边一个正把擤了鼻涕的手指往嘴里塞的男孩子 问 为什么他可以 女老师看了一眼又说 他是男孩子 男孩子脏点无所谓 王怡萍说 那我也要做男孩子 女老师噗嗤笑了 摸了摸王怡萍的小辫子 说 怡萍没法做男孩子的 怡萍以后长大是要当妈妈的 王怡萍瞪大了眼睛惊恐地说 我不要当妈妈 当妈妈要每天打扫好多卫生 我最讨厌做卫生了 而且妈妈都很无聊 什么都不知道 每次我问妈妈问题 她都让我去问爸爸 女老师无言以对 只能拍拍王怡萍的脑袋催促道 到了午睡的时间了 快去睡觉吧 王怡萍被按着肩膀跟在一队走得东倒西歪的小孩身后 走进一排排小床摆得整整齐齐散发着酸臭味的大屋子 那是汗味 牛奶 还有不知道谁的臭袜子和尿了的床单混杂一起的味道 很不好闻 但王怡萍已经习惯了 麻木地找到自己的小床躺下 哪怕她其实并不困 躺在床上 听着鼻炎小孩没完没了的吸鼻涕声 王怡萍呆呆地望着大玻璃窗外面的那颗巨大绿色杨树 心想 外面阳光和树叶那么新鲜 怎么就和我没关系呢

pages.left:
4. Zhang Che

The first time Wang Yiping met Zhang Che, it was raining.

She hadn’t brought an umbrella and walked down the empty street in a miserable state, stepping into puddles one after another. Her thin short skirt clung to her full but pale thighs.

She turned into a narrow alley. The buildings were so close together that the exhaust fans mounted on the walls barely formed a shelter from the rain. Yiping squeezed underneath one of them and stood trembling beside a filthy wall, pulling out a cigarette pack.

There was only one cigarette left in the crumpled paper.

She tried to light it several times but couldn’t. Just as she was cursing, a hand reached in from the side—thin, with veins protruding—holding a lighter.

She followed the hand and looked up.

A boy stood there, half a centimeter shorter than her, with a youthful face. He didn’t look much better than she did. His old black jacket was soaked through, clinging to the sharp ridge of his thin back.

The boy said,

“I’ve got fire. Let me have a drag.”

Yiping silently agreed.

She lit the cigarette using the flame from his hand and took a deep drag first. Along with the smoke she inhaled the stench of garbage in the alley and the smell of rain and exhaust.

Then she passed it to him.

Her faded red fingernails held the cigarette as the boy leaned forward and closed his lips around the slightly damp filter.

That year she met Zhang Che.

Zhang Che was fifteen.

She was twenty-three.
pages.right:
4. 张彻

王怡萍第一次遇见张彻的时候是个雨天。那天她没带伞，非常狼狈地走在没人的大街上，一脚一个水坑，薄薄的短裙紧贴着她丰腴又苍白的大腿。她走到一个狭窄的小巷，不合规的楼距让那些排气风扇成了勉强可以避雨的场所，王怡萍钻了进去，站在肮脏的墙壁旁边哆哆嗦嗦地掏出烟盒，皱巴巴的烟纸里只剩下最后一支了。王怡萍打了几次火都没打着，正在咒骂的时候斜刺伸过来一只手，凸出几根青筋的干瘦手背里攥着一个火机。王怡萍顺着看过去，一个比她矮半公分的男孩子，脸长得很嫩。同样的，他也好不到哪去，破旧的黑色夹克被雨水打湿，包裹着男孩子瘦得有些锋利的脊背骨。男孩子开口说，我有火，烟给我抽一口。王怡萍默然同意，她就着男孩子的手点燃烟，自己先深深吸了一口，连同一起吸进肺里的还有小巷里垃圾堆的臭味和雨水尾气。然后她递给男孩子，褪了色的红指甲夹着烟，男孩子把头伸过去含住微微濡湿的烟头。那一年她遇见了张彻，张彻十五岁，她二十三岁。

pages.left:
5. Record of Splendor

When Yiping worked at the Jinxiu Hotel giving foot massages, she could earn nearly two hundred yuan each time.

Of course it wasn’t exactly a legitimate massage—but it couldn’t be too obvious either. So while she kept her head down and worked in silence, the customers always groped her.

Yiping had long grown used to the hands of various men squeezing her breasts or pinching her thighs—fat hands, thin hands, stubby hands, knobby ones, yellowish ones, dark ones.

But there was one time she couldn’t stand.

A man touched her head.

Again and again.

Like a caress.

From her forehead slowly sliding to the white camellia hair tie around her ponytail. The heat of his palm was too intense. Yiping couldn’t help shivering.

Her face changed almost uncontrollably. Those dark, emotionless eyes—usually dull—hooked onto the man’s face like nails.

The man’s face was full of wrinkles. A pair of small eyes hid inside them, flickering under the dim yellow bedside lamp.

He took his hand off her head and pressed it against her cheek.

With a cheerful, ambiguous tone he said,

“You look just like my daughter.”

Yiping began trembling.

The man in front of her and the slippery feet submerged in the water she was holding seemed to swirl together into a giant vortex. The dim wall lamps grew brighter and brighter until they seemed almost burning, making her dizzy.

Yiping suddenly stood up and rushed into the bathroom. She dropped to her knees and clutched the toilet, vomiting violently.

Her stomach convulsed. Snot and tears poured out as she retched again and again. Her throat burned painfully.

Even after she finished vomiting she still clung to the toilet.

She didn’t even dare stand up.

It felt as if some monster outside the bathroom door would devour her whole.
pages.right:
5. 锦绣记

王怡萍在锦绣宾馆给人按脚那会儿，一次能挣小两百。当然不是正经按脚，但也不敢太明晃晃，所以一般她闷不吭声埋头苦干的时候那些客人总是对她上下其手。王怡萍早习惯了那些揉她胸脯掐她大腿的各种各样男人的手，胖的瘦的，短粗的嶙峋的，发黄发黑的。但她特受不了是有一次有个男的摸她头，一下一下，像爱抚。从她的额头慢慢滑到扎着马尾的白茶花头绳，那人手掌心的温度太烫了，让王怡萍没忍住抖了一下。她几乎是控制不住的脸色就变了，向来麻木没什么情绪的黑黢黢大眼睛钩子一样钉在那男人脸上。那男人脸上很多褶子，一对小眼就藏着那后头，床头壁灯的昏黄光线在里面一闪一闪。男人手从她头上拿下来，又贴在她脸颊上，语气愉快而暧昧，说，你长得真像我女儿。王怡萍开始抖，她觉得眼前的男人和她浸在水里的手握着那双滑溜溜的脚一起搅和在一起变成了一个大漩涡，那些暗淡的壁灯越来越亮，几乎烧起来，刺得她头晕目眩。王怡萍猛地站起来，一头扎进厕所里跪在地上抱着马桶呕得昏天黑地，胃部一个劲地抽搐，呕得鼻涕和眼泪都出来了。吐完了一轮又一轮，喉咙被烧得干疼，王怡萍还是抱着马桶不放，她甚至不敢站起来，就好像那厕所门外有什么怪物要把她吃干嚼碎咽进肚里了。

pages.left:
6. Habit

So what exactly was Zhang Che to Wang Yiping?

Sometimes he felt like a younger brother. He always asked naïve questions. In Yiping’s view, men were often more naïve than women in certain ways—and Zhang Che was just a kid.

But unlike most spoiled boys, nobody had raised Zhang Che to become a so-called “real man.” He didn’t even know what that meant.

So there was always a kind of indifference about him.

He didn’t care whether he was hungry or full. Didn’t care where he slept. Didn’t care about fighting or sex.

Most of his energy went into observing the world.

He had an intense desire to learn. Whenever he was around Yiping he asked endless questions—from her first menstruation to how many solar systems there were.

Sometimes Yiping threw a pillow at his face.

“Get lost.”

But usually she still answered patiently, even though many questions she didn’t know herself.

When she did this, she felt strangely calm.

She was like a soft, battered sponge squeezing out her own moisture to feed Zhang Che, who was always hungry for knowledge. His swollen curiosity filled her swollen desire to control—and vice versa.

The indifference they shared made the relationship easy.

Aside from sex, they were like an ordinary pair of siblings who didn’t get along very well.

Sometimes Zhang Che disappeared for days—so long Yiping nearly forgot he existed. Other times he would appear exactly when she reached a certain street corner.

He never greeted her.

He would squat under a road sign, watching her from afar. When she arrived, he stood up, grabbed her hand, and squeezed it—as if confirming she was real.

Then they would walk silently, one after the other, to some cheap hostel room and have sex.

Afterward came Zhang Che’s “ten thousand questions” time.

It seemed that during the days he disappeared he was collecting strange questions just to interrogate Yiping later.

Yet aside from asking questions, Zhang Che rarely spoke.

Anything that didn’t interest him never received a second glance.
pages.right:
6. 惯例

对于王怡萍来说，张彻到底是什么呢 他有时候像一个弟弟，总是问她一些很天真的问题，这没办法，在王怡萍看来，男人在某些方面总是比女人天真的，更何况张彻只是一个毛头小子。但是和世界上大部分被惯坏的毛头小子不同，张彻从小没有人教育他要像所谓男子汉一样，他甚至不知道那是什么东西。所以他的身上总有一种无所谓，无所谓饿着还是吃饱，无所谓睡在哪，也无所谓打架和性交。他把大部分的精力和时间都花在观察这个世界，他学习欲望很强，在王怡萍身边时总是问个不停，从她的月经初潮问到到底有几个太阳系。王怡萍有时候会把枕头甩到他脸上，说 滚蛋。但通常还是愿意耐心地解答张彻的问题，哪怕有很多她也不知道答案。只是在这样做的时候王怡萍感到很平静，她像一块软绵绵，又伤痕累累的大海绵，把自己的汁水挤给嗷嗷待哺的张彻，他格外膨胀的好奇心由她格外膨胀的控制欲填满，反之亦然。而她和张彻身上共同的那种无所谓，也让这段关系变得很轻松，他们除了做爱，就像一对普通的关系不大好的姐弟。张彻经常一连好几天不出现，久到王怡萍几乎要忘了他的存在，而有时候又会准确把握王怡萍出现在某个街角的时机，他从来不打招呼，蹲在路标下远远地望着她，等她来了，就站起来，抓住她的手捏一捏，似乎是确认她是否真实存在。然后他们就默契地一前一后走着，到随便一家招待所里开房，打炮。完事之后照例又是张彻的十万个为什么时间，似乎他不出现的日子里就是去搜罗各种千奇百怪的问题，然后一股脑地拿过来拷问王怡萍。但其实除了提问，张彻很少说话。他对自己不感兴趣的东西从不会看第二眼。

pages.left:
7. Jealousy

One time when Zhang Che came to see her, he had just finished a fight. A wound the size of a fingernail had split open on his scalp, blood gurgling slowly out of it. Half-coagulated streaks clung to his eyelashes and the steel hoop in his right ear. He hadn’t even bothered to wash.

Wang Yiping looked him up and down. The sight of him was honestly a little nauseating, so she grabbed a disposable towel and wiped him off a bit. While she was doing it, Zhang Che’s eyes kept staring at something golden in his hand, turning it over and over.

Yiping stopped. She leaned close to his ear and looked at the little gold thing in his palm.

“What’s that?”

Zhang Che leaned back. Yiping fell backward with him and sank into the bed, and he naturally pressed down onto her soft body like he was riding a small boat. He lifted the tiny golden object before his eyes.

“A tooth,” he said.

Yiping half-closed her eyes. She knew perfectly well this was probably just something Zhang Che had taken a sudden liking to and beaten someone up for. With a temperament like that, she wouldn’t be surprised if one day he simply disappeared from the world entirely. So far, though, luck had been on his side.

All she knew was that he worked odd jobs at bars. What exactly he did there, she had no idea.

She reached out and touched the steel ring in his ear. Watching how intently he stared at the gold tooth, she asked,

“Is it real?”

Zhang Che didn’t even think before answering.

“Don’t know.”

Yiping went quiet for two or three seconds. Then a surge of irritation rose in her chest. She shoved Zhang Che hard off of her, pushed and dragged him out the door, and before slamming it shut she threw the blood-soaked towel out after him.

After he left, Wang Yiping sat alone on the bed, staring into space.

She knew what it was.

She was just jealous.

Jealous that Zhang Che could be captivated by a single gold tooth.
Jealous that he didn’t care about anything else.

Didn’t care whether it was real or fake.
pages.right:
7. 嫉妒

有一回，张彻去找她的时候刚打完架，头上被开了个指甲盖儿大小的口子，正往外汩汩冒血。半凝固的血挂在他眼睫毛和右耳的钢圈上，洗都没洗一下。王怡萍左看右看，实在觉得他这幅样子太倒人胃口，就拿了条一次性毛巾给他擦了擦，擦的时候张彻的眼睛一直在盯着手里把玩的一颗金灿灿的玩意。王怡萍的动作停下，趴到他耳边看着他手里那颗像小金子一样的东西，问，这是什么。张彻往后一靠，王怡萍就倒下去陷进床里，他就理所当然地压在她柔软的身体上，像乘一艘小船。他举起来那颗小金子放到眼前，说，牙。王怡萍半合上眼，心里清楚这多半又是张彻一时兴起看上的东西，就把人给揍了。这样的性子她一点都不意外哪天张彻会彻底失踪，只不过目前为止他运气挺好。王怡萍只知道他在酒吧打杂，具体干些什么，她也不清楚。她把手伸过去摸他的耳朵上的钢圈，看他神情专注地一直在看那颗金牙，就问了一句，这是真的假的。张彻想也没想说，不知道。王怡萍安静了两三秒，心里陡生一股火气，猛地推了一把压在她身上的张彻，把人连轰带赶的推出房门，关门之前不忘把那条全是血的毛巾一起丢给他。张彻走了之后，王怡萍独自坐在床上发呆，她知道自己只是嫉妒了。嫉妒张彻能被一颗金牙吸引，嫉妒他根本不在乎旁的。不在乎真假。

pages.left:
8. Who

Yiping’er—

Yiping’er—

In Wang Yiping’s fragmented memories, it always seemed that someone was calling her. But she could never tell whether the voice was male or female, nor remember any emotion attached to it. All she remembered was that playful, slightly improper northern-style -er sound at the end.

Yiping’er.

Sometimes when Wang Yiping walked down the street she would suddenly turn around, certain someone had called her from behind.

But there was never anyone there.

Only a pale plastic trash bag drifting arrogantly across the hollow road behind her.

Yiping blinked in confusion, turned her head back, and continued walking slowly.

Strange, she thought.

I clearly heard someone call me.

Yiping’er—

She passed several streets, walked by the dirty moat surrounding the old city wall, and spat into the water. Kicking a small stone along the ground, she wandered into a park. There was no one around. She dropped onto the patchy yellow-green lawn, pulled the canvas bag she used to carry all sorts of junk over her face, and took a nap.

Half asleep, drifting between dream and waking, she heard it again.

Yiping’er—

A nameless irritation scratched up from inside her chest.

“Call your mother,” she muttered viciously.

But the voice didn’t fade.

Instead it came again and again beside her ear.

Yiping’er—
Yiping’er—
Yiping’er—

Wang Yiping suddenly ripped the bag off her face, sat up, and shouted into the empty park:

“Who?!”

The voice stopped.

A faint breeze slipped through the park, lightly playing with her hair. From far away came the dull metallic sounds of construction. The harsh midday sun illuminated the empty park in a lonely glare.

Yiping suddenly felt drained.

She fell back onto the grass, pouting with boredom.
pages.right:
8. 谁

怡萍儿——

怡萍儿——

在王怡萍断断续续的记忆里，似乎总是有人在呼唤着她。但是这声音她分不清楚男女，也想不起来有什么情绪，只是记得那个俏皮不太正经的儿化音，怡萍儿。

王怡萍有时候走在大街上，总会猛然回头，因为她感觉背后有人在叫她。但实际上那里什么都没有，一个白晃晃的垃圾袋大摇大摆地路过背后空洞的马路。王怡萍疑惑地眨眨眼，扭过头继续慢慢地走，她心想，奇了怪了，我分明听到有人喊我：怡萍儿——

她走过几条街，路过脏兮兮的护城河，站在那往河里吐了口痰；踢着脚下一颗石子走到一个公园里，四下无人，她就往黄绿相间的草坪上一倒，把自己那个用来装各种破烂的帆布袋子往脸上一盖。睡起了午觉。

恍恍惚惚半梦半醒间，她又听见有人喊：怡萍儿——

王怡萍一阵无名火挠起来，喊你妈x，她恶狠狠地骂。

这声音并没有因为她的痛骂而消散，反而在耳边一阵阵地，怡萍儿——怡萍儿——怡萍儿——

王怡萍猛地把帆布袋子掀开，往起一坐，冲四下无人的公园大喊一声：谁！

声音突然断了。微微风穿过公园，轻轻玩弄她的头发，远处工地打铁的声音若隐若现，大中午刺眼的太阳光把公园的空荡照耀得很孤单。王怡萍突然感觉卸了力，没精打采地往后一倒，无趣地撅起嘴。

pages.left:
9. Hobbies

The work Zhang Che did in bars was mostly the kind of shady business that preferred to stay unseen.

He was small, and his features were pale and clean. He looked like a high school kid who hadn’t even grown proper facial hair yet and had come out searching for excitement. Because of that, people often lowered their guard around him.

But bar owners had a good eye for talent.

They immediately recognized Zhang Che’s reckless nature and nimble hands.

After ten o’clock at night he would roam the bar street, slipping between neon-lit dens and smoky rooms, targeting the drunkest customers. In one night, a dozen wallets disappearing from that street would count as a slow evening.

Zhang Che was quiet and never bargained.

The bosses took eighty percent and left him twenty; he never complained. That alone made him easy to like.

Occasionally he would get caught in the act. When that happened, he never exposed the person who had sent him. He simply endured the owner’s rage silently, letting the victim beat him until his face bled.

That kind of loyalty earned him everyone’s trust.

Except for one strange habit.

Sometimes the bar owners would talk privately about it, clicking their tongues in disbelief.

“Kid’s probably got something wrong in the head.”

What kind of wrong?

If Zhang Che happened to see something he liked, he would grab it outright without caring about consequences.

For that reason, the bosses often deducted from his already meager pay.

The owner of Lily Bar on the corner once said:

“A guy came in looking for girls. When he opened his wallet there was a photo clipped inside—looked like his wife and daughter. Zhang Che saw it and suddenly wanted the picture. I mean, come on. You’re already a thief. If you want something, just take it quietly. Who asks for it straight to someone’s face?”

The customer had probably never seen someone so bizarre. He snapped immediately.

“Get lost, kid.”

But Zhang Che moved fast. He snatched the photo out, tossed the wallet onto the ground.

“If I hadn’t had people grab him and beat him in front of the guy to apologize,” the owner said, “I’d have lost business that night.”

“So what did he want the photo for?”

“What else? His mom died early, his dad doesn’t care about him. Probably saw a family of three and got jealous.”
pages.right:
9. 爱好

张彻在酒吧里干的活，尽是些偷偷摸摸不太光彩的事。因为个子小，又生得较为白净端正，看上去像是个毛没长齐就出来寻刺激的高中小孩。也因此让很多人放低了对他的警惕。

但是酒吧老板们还是慧眼识人，一眼就相中了张彻那种不要命的性子和他利索的手脚。于是，到了晚上十点往后，张彻就流窜于酒吧一条街上大大小小花红柳绿之地，专挑那些烂醉的人下手，一晚上那条街上的人能丢十几个钱包，这都算少的。

张彻沉默寡言，从不讨价还价，老板们跟他八二分成，他也没怨言，这点属实很讨人喜欢。偶尔有失手的时候被当场抓包，张彻也从不供出指使他干这事的人，一声不吭地让失主咬着牙红着脖子揍一顿，打得满头血。就冲这两点，就足以让张彻赢得所有老板们的信任。

除了偶尔他会犯轴。老板们有时会私下说起来，啧啧称奇，张彻这小子估计脑子多半有点毛病。怎么个毛病？要是让他瞅见喜欢的东西，就不管不顾地上去抢。为这事儿，老板们或多或少都克扣过他本来也不多的工资。上一回，街角丽丽酒吧的老板说道，有个客人来找妞儿，钱包摊开里头别了张照片儿，好像是和他闺女老婆。张彻这小子不知道看上人家照片啥了，上去就要。不是我说，你平时那偷鸡摸狗的事儿没少干，真想要暗自拿了不就行了，哪有直接跟人家要的。那客人估计也没见过这有病的，当下就恼火了，说滚一边儿去，小孩。然后这小子动作倒也快，一把抢了抽出来，还把人家钱包直接扔地上。要不是我赶紧让人逮了这小子当面揍一顿给人赔罪，我那晚上指不定得亏点。

你说，他拿人家照片干嘛。

还能干啥，妈死得早，爹不管他，看见一家三口，羡慕了呗。

pages.left:
10. The Little Braid

Zhang Che never cut his hair and never shaved.

The only time he cleaned himself up was when he met Wang Yiping and she dragged him into it.

Yiping had no special reason.

She simply didn’t want to sleep with a scruffy man.

Like eating off a plate—you should at least wipe it clean first.

Later she discovered something amusing.

Since Zhang Che’s hair had grown long, she started braiding it. First she twisted it into many thin little braids, then gathered them together with a hair tie into a small ponytail.

Zhang Che was naturally good-looking. Once she fixed his hair, the delicate lines of his pale face showed clearly. It looked surprisingly fresh.

Zhang Che let her do whatever she liked.

When he was with Yiping he behaved like a cloth doll most of the time. His eyes seemed to look at her, yet at the same time they were clearly gazing somewhere else.

While she braided his hair, he asked one question for each braid she finished.

If Yiping knew the answer she explained it seriously. If she didn’t, she made something up casually.

Zhang Che could always tell when she was lying.

“You’re making things up again,” he would say calmly.

Yiping would hold a rubber band between her teeth, click her tongue, and smack the back of his head lightly.

“Stop moving.”

When she finished the final braid, Zhang Che asked:

“Why do some women wear pants underneath their skirts?”

Yiping burst into laughter. She grabbed his shoulders and turned him around to admire her work.

“To cover something that’s already obvious,” she said.

Zhang Che frowned.

“What does ‘cover something obvious’ mean?”

Yiping imitated the arrogant villains from television dramas. She took a drag from her cigarette and lightly pinched his chin, turning his face left and then right as if examining him.

Satisfied, she said,

“Look it up in a dictionary yourself.”

Then she stuffed the cigarette, almost burned down to the filter, between Zhang Che’s slightly pursed lips.
pages.right:
10. 小辫儿

张彻从来不去理发，也不刮胡子，都是和王怡萍见面的时候被她揪着捯饬干净的。

王怡萍倒也没什么特别的理由，单纯不想和胡子拉碴的男人搞。就像吃饭的盘子至少得擦干净一样。

后来她发现个好玩的，由于张彻的头发留得长了，她干脆给他编起了小辫，先扎成一根一根的小麻花，再把这一把小麻花拿个头绳捆起来扎成马尾。张彻长得不差，这样一弄，那张标准小白脸都露出来了，瞅着倒也挺新鲜。

张彻就随她去弄，他和王怡萍在一块的大部分时间都表现得像个布娃娃，那双眼睛好像在看着王怡萍却又明明在望着别处。王怡萍给他扎小辫的时候，弄完一根张彻就问她一个问题，王怡萍知道的认真说说，不知道的就随口糊弄，张彻一听就知道她在胡说，倒也不较真，平静地说，你又瞎说。王怡萍叼着根皮绳，啧了一声轻轻拍他脑袋一巴掌，别乱动。

王怡萍扎完最后一根辫子的时候，张彻问她：为什么有的女人在裙子下面要再穿条裤子？

王怡萍咯咯笑了，一边扶着他的肩膀把他扭过来欣赏自己的作品，一边说，欲盖弥彰。

张彻问，玉盖迷张是什么东西？

王怡萍学着电视剧里那些“总裁”坏男人的模样，一边吸了口烟一边轻佻地捏住张彻的下巴，把他的脸扭到左边又扳回右边，似乎很满意地说，你自己查新华词典去。然后把快烧到屁股的烟塞进张彻抿着的嘴唇中间。

pages.left:
11. A Sense of Awe

There were certain women Wang Yiping was afraid of.

Especially groups of female students chatting and laughing together.

She wasn’t afraid of men.

Men, in her eyes, were like factory products. Whether tall or short, fat or thin, beneath their outer shells they always followed roughly the same logic.

And she didn’t fear things that were “roughly the same.”

Would you be afraid of pigeons chasing corn kernels in a square?

The men walking the streets around her looked no different from the rows of silent poplar trees along the roadside.

But women were different.

Some women made Yiping feel something strange—even though she herself was a woman.

Driven by a curious instinct inherited from generations of ancestors, Yiping sometimes went deliberately to the gates of middle schools or universities. She would squat nearby and watch the female students leaving campus, sometimes for an entire afternoon.

After observing long enough, she arrived at a clumsy academic conclusion.

Most of those female students had never had a penis in their mouths.

Under their skirts there was emptiness.

Two sleeves filled only with clean wind.

A gaping mouth capable of swallowing the whole world.

They seemed able to exist simply by gossiping about celebrities and buying pretty little plastic trinkets.

For Yiping, that kind of existence felt so distant it was almost unreal.

But her fear of them was not contempt.

Quite the opposite.

She was like a tiger in a zoo cage.

While she felt proud for snatching a few extra pieces of meat from the keeper that day, the visitors outside the iron bars only laughed and said:

“Look—the tiger’s taking a nap.”
pages.right:
11. 敬畏之心

怡萍对于某一些女人是害怕的，尤其是那些三五成群说说笑笑的女学生。

她并不怕男人，因为男人在她看来像是工厂批量生产出来的，在他们高或矮，胖或瘦的外皮下，总是遵循着一套差不多的逻辑。她可不害怕“差不多”，难道你会惧怕广场上一群永远在追逐着游客手里的玉米粒的鸽子吗？这些差不多先生出现在她周围的街道上，和路旁沉默排列的杨树是差不多的光景。

可是，女人很不一样。总有那么一些女人，让同为女人的王怡萍产生异样的感觉。

出于老祖宗教导下的格物致知精神，王怡萍时不时会专门去中学或者大学的门口，蹲着观察那些走出来的女学生，经常一看就是一个下午。

看得多了，王怡萍学士艰难地得出了一条不甚高明的结论：大概是因为，那大多数的女学生没含过屌。

她们的裙子下是空空荡荡，是两袖清风，是一张可以吞噬一切的血盆大口。

她们似乎可以依靠谈论明星的花边新闻和购买形状漂亮的塑料小玩意存在于这个世界上。这对王怡萍来说，是一种太过于遥远以至于怀疑其真实性的体会。但是王怡萍的这种恐惧，并不是出于鄙薄这些女学生，恰恰相反，她就像是动物园里那个被关在铁网之内的老虎狮子，在她为今天从饲养员手里多抢了几块肉而沾沾自喜时，铁网之外那些围观她的路人只会笑着说，瞧，那只老虎在打盹呢。

pages.left:
12. The First Time

One time Zhang Che talked about his family.

“I’m not going back.”

“Why?”

“I ran away.”

“Only once?”

Zhang Che looked at her and nodded, pulling a cigarette from behind her ear.

“You bastard. I should discipline you for your father!”

Yiping exploded with anger, grabbing his collar and slapping him rapidly.

Zhang Che only raised an arm slightly to block her, then let her continue.

She hit hard.

The red marks of her fingers burned on his skin. His thin old nylon jacket crumpled like paper in her hands as she shook him back and forth, her fists pounding his hot face like kneading a lump of clay.

The room filled with a percussion of blows.

Thump. Thump. Smack. Smack.

Occasionally the sharp rip of fabric tearing.

Yiping stopped when she saw blood slowly running from Zhang Che’s nose.

It dripped onto the hem of her white dress and spread out into a vulgar red flower.

Zhang Che reached under the pillow and retrieved the cigarette he had hidden there earlier. It had been crushed, loose tobacco scattered across the bed.

He gently blew the crumbs away and held it up before Yiping’s dejected face.

“Have a smoke.”

The percussion had ended.

The sound of the lighter clicking and tobacco sizzling marked the finale.

They sat facing each other, sharing one cigarette as pale smoke drifted upward between them.

“I was jealous of you just now,” Yiping said.

“But you succeeded too,” Zhang Che replied. “You ran away.”

“But the first time I tried, I only made it to the bottom of my apartment building.”

She took the half-smoked cigarette from his hand and inhaled deeply.

“I sat in the stairwell hugging my knees, listening nervously to footsteps outside. I was scared—and hoping someone would come.”

“You’re not there anymore.”

“But the first time is the most important!”

Yiping stood up abruptly. Ash fell in soft flakes.

“As long as the first attempt fails, then no matter what happens later, failure is still failure.”

The cigarette had almost burned out.

Zhang Che took it from her fingers and crushed it in his palm.

The twisted butt fell to the floor.

With his ash-stained hand he held her wrist, gently rocking it back and forth.

Yiping felt the place where he touched her.

It was very warm.
pages.right:
12. 第一次

有一回，张彻对她说起家里的事。

“不回去了。”

“为什么？”

“逃了。”

“只逃过一次？”

张彻看着她，点点头，从她的耳朵后面取下一支烟。

”混蛋，我要替你爸爸教训你！“怡萍说着，怒而暴起，抓着张彻的衣领子巴掌如急雨落下。

张彻只举起手稍微阻挡了一下，就随她去了。怡萍打得很重，鲜红的五个手指头滚烫，张彻那件薄而旧的尼龙外套在她手里像团废纸，怡萍把他晃来晃去，拳头砸在热滚滚的脸上，像在锤一团结实的泥巴。房间里响起咚、咚、啪、啪的打击乐，时不时有衣服被撕拉的脆响。

怡萍不打了，因为她看到了从张彻鼻子下面缓慢流淌的血。啪嗒一下，在怡萍的白色连衣裙摆上散开，晕成一朵俗艳的花。

张彻从枕头下面掏出刚才那根烟，被揉皱了，漏出来的烟草碎屑粘在床上。张彻小心地吹了吹，在垂头丧气的怡萍眼前举起来。”抽烟吧。“

打击乐已经平息，以打火机的咔嚓声和烟草点燃的滋滋作响作为结束。怡萍和张彻面对面坐着，共享一支烟，乳白色的烟雾在两人中间升腾而起。

”我刚才是嫉妒你。“

”你不是也成功了吗？逃家。“

”但是我第一次的时候，只逃到了我家楼底下。“

怡萍从张彻手里接过燃了一半的香烟，嘬了一大口。“就坐在公寓的楼道里，双手抱着膝盖，紧张地听着从外面传来的脚步，害怕又期待。”

“你现在已经不在那了。”

“但是第一次是最重要的！”

怡萍站起来，烟灰簌簌脱落，张彻仰起头看着她。

“只要第一次失败了，之后不管怎样，失败了就是失败了。”

烟要烧到尽头了，张彻从她两指之间取下来，在手心里掐灭。捏成一团的烟头掉在地上，张彻用沾了烟灰的手掌握住怡萍的腕子，一前一后轻轻晃动起来。怡萍只觉得，张彻抓住她的地方挺烫。

pages.left:
13. The Crane

In Yiping’s mind, cranes were the execution platforms of the twenty-first century.

The square wooden gallows of the Middle Ages were dignified enough.

But cranes were larger.

Higher.

More ruthless.

Whenever a crane lifted a massive slab of stone, Yiping couldn’t help imagining that the hook should really be hanging from someone’s neck—or that beneath the stone there lay a row of people waiting to die.

She liked watching cranes at work at night.

Industrial floodlights made the moonlight worthless. Rows of harsh white light lined up like a stage prepared for a superstar.

The crane stretched its arm outward.

Its crossed steel body resembled a cannon aimed into space—or perhaps a crucifix forged from iron.

When it worked, it was mostly silent.

Only when the arm rotated or when a heap of steel dropped down did it release a heavy roar. Dust rose into the cold white light like the breath of some enormous beast.

Night-working cranes were the city’s great animals.

These animals spent midnight hours building tombstones for sleeping people.

Concrete.
Rebar.
Glass.
Granite.
Lime.

Yiping stared blankly at the magnificent arm of the crane sweeping through the night sky.

She lit a cigarette and inhaled deeply, drawing smog, dust, nicotine, and alkaloids together into her body, which was producing oxytocin in violent surges.
pages.right:
13.起重机

在怡萍心里，起重机才是二十一世纪的行刑台。

中世纪那种方方正正的门框形状当然不错，足够严肃。但是起重机更大，更高，更冷酷。

当起重机吊起一大块石砖的时候，怡萍总情不自禁地幻想，那个钩子应该吊的是某人的后颈，或者，在石砖下面已经躺好了一排等死的人。

她喜欢在夜晚观看工作的起重机，工业照明的强光让月色不值一提，整肃的白光一排排齐刷刷，像是给大明星准备好的舞台。起重机伸长了手臂，十字交叉的身姿像是一门向宇宙发射的大炮，又或者是钢铁铸成的十字架。起重机工作的时候是沉默的，只有在偶然转动吊臂或者将一大堆钢材放下的时候才发出一声沉闷的轰鸣，扬起的尘在生冷的白光里获得生命，像是巨兽的吐息。

夜晚工作的起重机，是城市里的大动物。

这个大动物在深夜为熟睡的人们建起一座又一座墓碑，用水泥，钢筋，玻璃，花岗岩，石灰。

怡萍痴呆呆地望着起重机雄伟的吊臂在夜空下挥斥方遒，她点起一根烟，深深地吸气，连同雾霾粉尘和尼古丁碱一块儿，灌进她正猛烈分泌催产素的身体。

pages.left:
14. Are You Lonely?

One time Yiping stood under a streetlight, lost in thought.

Often this was how she spent her days—turning herself into an unnoticed part of the city’s scenery.

A young couple walked past.

The girl asked the boy beside her,

“Are you lonely?”

Yiping didn’t hear the boy’s answer.

They laughed and walked away quickly, their voices swallowed by sharp car horns and the rumbling of a passing bus.

Are you lonely?

Yiping repeated the question silently in her mind.

She dropped those four words into the well of her thoughts.

It was very deep.

The echo never came back.

She opened her palm.

Her left ring finger had once been injured; the joint had twisted slightly and could never fully straighten again. It trembled faintly.

She stared at the lines in her palm and murmured to herself:

“Not lonely.”

She spoke to herself many times.

Her own voice was like the first cigarette in the morning—it calmed her, steadied her quickly.

So Yiping believed:

I’m not lonely.

She felt like a seed caught in a rolling tumbleweed—somehow it had grown, broken out of its shell, stretched taller and taller, but never formed roots.

Just like now, standing idly by the roadside. People brushed past her without paying attention. At most they glanced at her strangely.

Probably because Yiping didn’t play with her phone or talk to anyone. She simply stood there quietly, looking straight ahead.

Standing here, the thing she felt closest to in the world

was the fire hydrant beside her.
pages.right:
14.你孤独吗？

有一次怡萍站在大街上，路灯底下发呆。很多时候她就是这样过日子的，把自己活成城市里不起眼更不靓丽的一道无人在意的风景线。

一对年轻人男女从她旁边路过，女孩问旁边那人，你孤独吗？

怡萍没听清男孩回答了什么，两人说说笑笑很快就走远了，模糊不清的话语被急促的汽笛声和公交车行驶的轰响淹没。

你孤独吗？

怡萍默默地在心里又问了一遍这个问题。她对着脑海里的声音陷入沉思，这四个字被抛下去，井可真深，一直都等不到落下的回声。

怡萍把手掌摊开，她左手的无名指以前受了点伤，指关节变形后无法完全伸展，此时正微微颤抖着。她凝视着自己掌心的脉络，喃喃自语道：“不孤独。”有很多次，她都对自己说话。自己说的话就像早上起来抽的第一根烟，让她平静，很快稳定下来。所以怡萍认为，我不孤独。

她感觉自己就像一颗夹在风滚草里的种子，不知怎得就长大了，破壳而出，越长越高，但总是没有根的。就像她天天无事站在这街边，路上擦肩而过很多人都不把她当回事儿，最多奇怪地看她两眼，大抵是因为怡萍不玩手机，也不和人说话，就这么静静地站着，目视前方。怡萍站在这儿，感觉和自己关系最近的是旁边那根消防栓。
